title: Certification and the MT Process Overview
narrationScript: Magnetic particle testing is simple to demonstrate and easy to do badly. Industry's answer to that risk is a formal system of training, qualification, and certification, and you are inside that system right now. Let us define the words first, because they are not interchangeable. Training is organized instruction in the principles and practice of the method, the formal hours this course provides. Qualification is demonstrated capability, training plus documented experience plus passing the required examinations and vision checks. Certification is the written testimony, issued by your employer or a central body, that you are qualified. In the United States, the most common framework is SNT-TC-1A, a recommended practice published by the American Society for Nondestructive Testing. Recommended is the key word. It is not a law and not a standard. Each employer writes its own controlling document, called a written practice, that adapts the recommendations into hard requirements for that company. The training hours, the experience hours, the examinations, the passing grades. So when someone asks what you need to reach Level Two in MT, the honest answer is always, read the written practice. Where contracts demand a standard rather than a recommendation, companies work to ANSI ASNT CP-189, which sets minimum requirements that cannot be tailored downward. Aerospace runs on NAS 410, which is generally stricter about hours, examinations, and documentation. And central certification schemes, such as ASNT's central certification program and the international ISO 9712 system, qualify and certify you through a central body rather than through your employer. The three level structure is common to all of these documents. A trainee works only under direct supervision and makes no accept or reject decisions. A Level One performs specific calibrations and specific tests under written instructions and records the results, but does not independently interpret or evaluate. A Level Two sets up and standardizes equipment, performs tests, and, this is the heart of it, interprets indications and evaluates them against codes, standards, and specifications, then signs for the result. A Level Three establishes techniques and procedures, interprets codes and standards, designates the methods and techniques to be used, and carries responsibility for training and examining others. Moving up that ladder stacks three things. Formal training hours. Documented experience hours earned under supervision. And examinations, normally three of them. A general examination on the theory of the method. A specific examination on your employer's procedures, equipment, and specifications. And a practical examination where you demonstrate real tests on real parts while an examiner scores you against a checklist. A composite grade of at least eighty percent is the common passing requirement. Your eyes are part of qualification too. Near vision acuity is verified, typically every year, because an inspector who cannot resolve fine detail cannot find fine indications. Color perception is checked as well, to be sure you can distinguish the colors and contrasts the method depends on. And certification is not permanent. Recertification typically runs on a three to five year cycle depending on the document and the level, and certification can be suspended or revoked for cause. Now let us walk the test itself from end to end, so the rest of this course has a skeleton to hang on. Every magnetic particle test follows the same backbone. First, know the job. The governing procedure tells you the part, the technique, the equipment, and the acceptance criteria. Second, prepare the surface. Clean off grease, loose scale, loose rust, and any coating that would hold particles away from the surface or hide them. Third, verify your system. Equipment checks, particle condition, and lighting. White light intensity for visible particles, ultraviolet light intensity and a darkened area for fluorescent ones. Fourth, magnetize the part in a chosen first direction. Fifth, apply the particles while the magnetizing current is flowing, which is called the continuous method, and watch the indications form. Sixth, interpret what you see. Decide what is a real indication of a discontinuity and what is not, and evaluate the real ones against the acceptance criteria. Seventh, magnetize again, roughly perpendicular to the first direction, and repeat the application and interpretation, because a single direction can completely miss a flaw that lies along the flux. Eighth, finish the job. Record the results, demagnetize if the part or the next operation requires it, and clean the residue off the part. Preparation, magnetization, application, interpretation, documentation. Five words, one method. Everything that follows in this course is detail hung on that frame, and by the end of it each of those five words will be a skill you can demonstrate under an examiner's eye.
imagePrompt: An examiner with a clipboard observes a young technician performing a magnetic particle yoke test on a structural steel weld in a training lab, dry red powder dusted along the weld toe between the yoke legs.

sections.heading: Training, Qualification, Certification
sections.body:
Three words anchor this lesson, and they are not interchangeable.

- **Training** is organized instruction in the principles and practice of the method - the formal classroom hours this course provides.
- **Qualification** is demonstrated capability: required training **plus** documented experience **plus** passing the required examinations and vision checks.
- **Certification** is written testimony that a person is qualified - issued by the employer in most U.S. programs, or by a central body in central certification schemes.

A **trainee** is a person working toward initial qualification: they work under direct supervision and make no independent accept/reject calls. The distinction matters legally and contractually. When a Level II signs a report, that signature asserts that a qualified, certified individual interpreted and evaluated the results. Customers audit certification records for exactly this reason, and a lapsed eye exam or missing experience documentation can void test reports - not because the inspector forgot how to inspect, but because the *evidence* of qualification failed.

Keep your own copies of training certificates, experience logs, and vision records. Technicians change employers, and employer-based certification does not transfer: the new employer must certify you under its own written practice. Complete personal records make that process fast instead of painful.

sections.heading: The Documents That Govern Qualification
sections.body:
Four families of documents govern MT personnel qualification, and you should know which one you are working under:

- **SNT-TC-1A** - a *recommended practice* published by ASNT. It provides guidelines that each employer adapts into a **written practice**: the company document that fixes the actual training hours, experience hours, examinations, and grades. Under SNT-TC-1A, the employer certifies its own personnel, and the written practice is the controlling document.
- **ANSI/ASNT CP-189** - a true American National *Standard*. Where a contract invokes CP-189, its minimum requirements cannot be tailored downward the way a recommended practice can.
- **NAS 410** - the aerospace standard, used by airframe and engine primes and their suppliers. Generally more prescriptive about training hours, examinations, and documentation.
- **Central certification** - schemes such as ASNT's central certification program and **ISO 9712**, in which a central body (not the employer) examines and certifies the individual.

Typical numbers - on the order of a dozen formal training hours for MT Level I, additional hours for Level II, and months of supervised experience - vary by document and employer. The exact figures binding *you* come from your employer's written practice or the governing standard, never from memory.

sections.caption: The qualification ladder from trainee to Level III, showing what stacks at each step: training, experience, examinations, and vision checks.
The three certification levels and what each may do.
rows: Works under direct supervision while accumulating training and experience
No independent tests, no accept/reject decisions
Performs specific calibrations and tests per written instructions; records results
Does not independently interpret or evaluate results
Sets up and standardizes equipment, performs tests, interprets and evaluates per codes and specifications, reports results
Works within certified method and techniques; guided by procedures approved at Level III
Establishes techniques and procedures, interprets codes and standards, designates methods, trains and examines personnel
Carries responsibility for the technical adequacy of the whole program

sections.heading: Examinations, Vision, and Staying Certified
sections.body:
Qualification examinations normally come in three parts:

- **General** - the theory of the method: magnetism, equipment, particles, techniques.
- **Specific** - your employer's procedures, equipment, and the specifications you work to.
- **Practical** - hands-on demonstration: you perform actual examinations on representative parts while an examiner scores you against a checklist, including detection of known discontinuities.

A **composite grade of at least 80 percent** (with minimums on each exam) is the common passing requirement. Vision is examined too: **near-vision acuity**, verified on a standard chart at a defined reading distance, is typically required **annually**, and **color perception** adequate to distinguish the contrasts the method uses is verified at qualification and periodically after. Certification then runs on a clock: **recertification on a three-to-five-year cycle** is typical, by examination or by evidence of continuing satisfactory performance, depending on the governing document - and certification can be suspended or revoked for cause, such as falsified reports. An extended interruption of service in the method can likewise require reexamination before certification is reinstated. None of this is bureaucratic decoration. It is the chain of evidence that lets a customer trust a signature on a test report.

sections.title: The written practice governs
sections.body: Training hours, experience hours, exam content, passing grades, vision intervals, recertification - the binding numbers come from **your employer's written practice** (under SNT-TC-1A) or the invoked standard (CP-189, NAS 410, ISO 9712). When this course says "typically," your written practice says *exactly*. Read it.

sections.heading: The MT Process from Start to Finish
sections.body:
Here is the whole method in one pass - the skeleton every later lesson hangs detail on:

1. **Know the job.** The governing procedure defines the part, technique, equipment, coverage, and acceptance criteria.
2. **Prepare the surface.** Remove grease, oil, loose scale and rust, and coatings that would hold particles off the surface or mask indications.
3. **Verify the system.** Equipment performance checks, particle or bath condition, and lighting - adequate white light for visible particles; ultraviolet intensity and a darkened area for fluorescent ones.
4. **Magnetize - first direction.** Establish a magnetic field of suitable strength and known direction in the part.
5. **Apply particles.** In the standard *continuous method*, particles are applied while magnetizing current flows, and indications form over leakage fields.
6. **Interpret and evaluate.** Decide which indications are relevant, then evaluate relevant ones against the acceptance criteria.
7. **Magnetize - second direction.** Repeat application and interpretation with flux roughly perpendicular to the first shot, because a discontinuity lying parallel to the flux may not show at all.
8. **Close out.** Record results, demagnetize when required, and post-clean the part.

Preparation, magnetization, application, interpretation, documentation - five words that contain the entire method.

sections.caption: The eight-step magnetic particle testing sequence, with the two roughly perpendicular magnetizations that complete coverage.

figures.title: The MT process, start to finish

checkQuestions.question: What kind of document is SNT-TC-1A, and what document actually fixes the qualification requirements at a company working under it?
options: A federal law; the company's union agreement fixes the requirements
A recommended practice; the employer's written practice fixes the requirements
A mandatory international standard; ISO 9712 fixes the requirements
A purchase specification; the customer's drawing fixes the requirements
checkQuestions.explanation: SNT-TC-1A is a recommended practice. Each employer adapts it into a written practice, which is the controlling document for training hours, experience, exams, and grades at that company.

checkQuestions.question: Which certification level is the lowest that may independently interpret indications, evaluate them against codes and specifications, and report results?
options: Trainee
Level I
Level II
Only Level III
checkQuestions.explanation: Interpretation and evaluation against acceptance criteria define Level II responsibility. A Level I performs tests per written instructions and records results but does not independently evaluate.

checkQuestions.question: An aerospace engine overhaul contract requires personnel qualified to NAS 410. Compared with SNT-TC-1A, NAS 410 is best described as:
options: An identical document published under a different cover
A more prescriptive aerospace standard with stricter training, examination, and documentation requirements
A recommended practice that each supplier may relax in its written practice
A standard that applies only to Level III personnel
checkQuestions.explanation: NAS 410 is the aerospace personnel qualification standard and is generally more prescriptive than the SNT-TC-1A recommended practice; its requirements are not tailorable downward the way recommendations are.

checkQuestions.question: In the qualification examination set, which exam requires the candidate to demonstrate actual tests on representative parts while being scored against a checklist?
options: The general examination
The specific examination
The practical examination
The vision examination
checkQuestions.explanation: The practical exam is the hands-on demonstration, including detection of known discontinuities; the general exam covers method theory and the specific exam covers employer procedures and specifications.

checkQuestions.question: In the standard continuous method, when are the magnetic particles applied?
options: Before the part is magnetized, so they can settle into discontinuities
While the magnetizing current is flowing
Only after the current is switched off and the part holds a residual field
During demagnetization, as the field collapses
checkQuestions.explanation: The continuous method applies particles while magnetizing current flows, when the field is strongest - it is the standard, most sensitive timing. Applying after current off is the residual method, used only on high-retentivity parts.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §1.4 (training and certification process)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §1.5 (MT process overview)

ANSI/ASNT CP-189 (standard for qualification and certification context)
